coredata.dc:title: Inexpensive and green electrochemical sensor for the determination of Cd(II) and Pb(II) by square wave anodic stripping voltammetry in bivalve mollusks
coredata.dc:creator: Pizarro, Jaime
Segura, Rodrigo
Tapia, Diego
Navarro, Freddy
Fuenzalida, Francesca
Jesús Aguirre, María
coredata.dc:description: An inexpensive and environmental friendly electrochemical sensor based on a glassy carbon electrode (GC) modified with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and Nafion (NF) has been developed for the determination of Cd(II) and Pb(II) in bivalve mollusks using square wave anodic stripping voltammetry (SWASV). GQDs were characterized by UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence and transmission electronic microscopy (TEM). The modified electrode was evaluated by cyclic voltammetry, scanning electronic microscopy (SEM) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). A linearity of 20–200 μg L−1 was found, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 11.30 μg L−1 for Cd(II) and 8.49 μg L−1 for Pb(II). The proposed methodology was validated with a certified reference material TMDA-64.2. The reproducibility of GC/GQDs-NF for both species had an RSD of less than 10%. The results were compared with ICP-OES. The method was applied in the determination of Cd(II) and Pb(II) in bivalve mollusks samples with excellent results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Nafion
Electrochemical sensor
Square wave anodic stripping voltammetry
Cadmium
Lead

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis, characterization of GQDs and characterization of electrode surfaces by SEM
Characterization of modified surfaces by cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)
Optimization of chemical and electrochemical parameters
Electrochemical behavior of Cd(II) and Pb(II) and analytical validation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Comisión Nacional de Investigación Científica y Tecnológica
Fondo Nacional de Desarrollo Científico y Tecnológico

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank FONDECYT Chile, for financial support under projects N° 1180804 (RS), 1160324 (MJA), “Comisión Nacional de Investigación Científica y Tecnológica” ( CONICYT ) for doctoral fellowship 21150868 (FN) and to the project 1145-USA 1799-1-1-3 VRIDEI, Universidad de Santiago de Chile.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis spectrum of citric acid and GQDs, (b) fluorescence spectrum of GQDs, (c) TEM image of GQDs and SEM images of (d) GC, (e) GC/NF (0.5 wt%), and (f) GC/GQDs-NF (0.5 wt%).

Cyclic voltammograms of 1 mmol L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/[Fe(CN)6]4− in 0.1 mol L−1 KCl in GC, GC/NF (0.5 wt%), and GC/GQDs-NF (0.5 wt%). Scanning rate: 0.1 V s−1. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of electrode modification process measured in 1 mmol L−1 de [Fe(CN)6]3−/[Fe(CN)6]4− (1:1) and 0.1 mol L−1 KCl. (b) shows Nyquist diagram at OCP of bare (GC), GC/NF and GC/GQDs-NF electrodes.

Effect of: (a) supporting electrolyte on the stripping signal of 40 µg L−1 of Pb(II) and Cd(II) using GC/GQDs-NF (0.5 wt%, NF). Electrochemical parameters (Eacc = −1.3 V and tacc = 120 s. (b) Effect of Nafion concentration on the stripping signal of 40 µg L−1 of Pb(II) and Cd(II) using GC/GQDs-NF. Electrochemical parameters (Eacc = −1.3, tacc = 120 s and sodium formate Buffer, pH = 4.5). (c) Effect of pH concentration on the stripping signal of 40 µg L−1 of Pb(II) and Cd(II) using GC/GQDs-NF (0.5 wt%, NF). Electrochemical parameters (Eacc = −1.3 and tacc = 120 s). (d) Effect of Eacc on the stripping signal of 40 µg L−1 of Pb(II) and Cd(II) with a tacc of 120 s, using GC/GQDs-NF (0.5 wt% NF) (sodium formate, pH = 4.5). (e) Effect of tacc on the stripping signal of 40 µg L−1 of Pb(II) and Cd(II) using GC/GQDs-NF (0.5 wt% NF) with a Eacc of −1.3 V using a pH 4.5.

Electrochemical behavior of 40 µg L−1 Cd(II) and Pb(II) in GC, GC/NF (0.5 wt%) and GC/GQDs-NF (0.5 wt%). Formate buffer 0.1 mol L−1 (pH 4.5). Optimized electrochemical parameters: Eacc = −1.3 V and tacc = 150 s. (b) Square wave anodic stripping voltammograms and calibration curves for Cd(II) and Pb(II) using sodium formate buffer 0.1 mol L−1 (pH 4.5). Optimized electrochemical parameters: Eacc = −1.3 V and tacc = 150 s.

Plot of standard addition method for the determination of Cd(II) and Pb(II) in analytical reference material (TMDA-64.2) using GC/GQDs-NF. Optimized electrochemical parameters: Eacc = −1.3 V and tacc = 150 s.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Selected data reported for the analysis of Cd(II) and Pb(II) using graphene-based nanomaterials modified electrodes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Nafion-G: Graphene nanosheets dispersed in Nafion; SPE/ERGNO: Screen printed electrode/Electrochemically reduced graphene oxide; RGO-CS/PLL/GCE: Reduced graphene oxide-Chitosan/poly-l-lysine films/Glassy carbon electrode.

head.ce:title: Inexpensive and green electrochemical sensor for the determination of Cd(II) and Pb(II) by square wave anodic stripping voltammetry in bivalve mollusks

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Química de los Materiales, Facultad de Química y Biología, Universidad de Santiago de Chile (USACH), Santiago 3363, Chile

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Departamento de Química de los Materiales

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Departamento de Química de los Materiales, Facultad de Química y Biología, Universidad de Santiago de Chile (USACH), Santiago 3363, Chile

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An inexpensive and environmental friendly electrochemical sensor based on a glassy carbon electrode (GC) modified with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and Nafion (NF) has been developed for the determination of Cd(II) and Pb(II) in bivalve mollusks using square wave anodic stripping voltammetry (SWASV). GQDs were characterized by UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence and transmission electronic microscopy (TEM). The modified electrode was evaluated by cyclic voltammetry, scanning electronic microscopy (SEM) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). A linearity of 20–200 μg L−1 was found, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 11.30 μg L−1 for Cd(II) and 8.49 μg L−1 for Pb(II). The proposed methodology was validated with a certified reference material TMDA-64.2. The reproducibility of GC/GQDs-NF for both species had an RSD of less than 10%. The results were compared with ICP-OES. The method was applied in the determination of Cd(II) and Pb(II) in bivalve mollusks samples with excellent results.

ce:section.ce:para: During the last years, the different industrial activities have expanded the distribution of heavy metals in the environment, generating adverse effects on ecosystems and human resources health (Flores et al., 2017; Garrett, 2000; Mendoza-Carranza, Sepúlveda-Lozada, Dias-Ferreira, & Geissen, 2016; Pizarro, Flores et al., 2019; Sharma & Agrawal, 2005). In this context, there is an environmental problem associated with the accumulation of heavy metals in hydrobiological resources such as bivalve mollusks, which depends, among other factors, on the concentration and bioavailability of pollutants in seawater (Alloway, 1995; Zeng, Xu, Boezen, & Huo, 2016). Nowadays, the consumption of marine resources (bivalve mollusks) capable of accumulating two of the most important polluting agents (Cd and Pb) in their tissues and organs (Maanan, 2008; Rainbow, 2002) has grown significantly. In this sense, it becomes urgent to implement solutions of low cost and high reliability that allow monitoring the quality of products of high commercial demand such as marine resources.

Among the most commonly used techniques to determine Cd(II) and Pb(II) are spectroscopic techniques such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) (Khalifa, Hamil, Al-Houni, & Ackacha, 2010; Psoma, Pasias, Rousis, Barkonikos, & Thomaidis, 2014), inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) (Hamilton, Rode, Merchant, & Sneddon, 2008; Sen, Shandil, & Shrivastava, 2011), and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (Guérin et al., 2011). Despite the high sensitivity and reproducibility of spectroscopic techniques, its implementation is expensive. In contrast, stripping techniques such as square wave anodic stripping voltammetry (SWASV) present high sensitivity, reproducibility, portability and low cost (Batley & Florence, 1974; Flores et al., 2017; Segura et al., 2015; Wang, Lu, Hocevar, Farias, & Ogorevc, 2000). Nowadays, modern voltammetric techniques are highly influenced by the development of modified electrodes that lead to an increase in preconcentration, catalytic effects and the ability to eliminate interferers, providing a viable alternative for the development of electrochemical sensors.

The voltammetric determination of Cd(II) and Pb(II) has been explored in various works, some reports indicate the use of a hanging mercury drop electrode (HMDE) (Grabarczyk & Wardak, 2016) and others are centered on the incorporation of modifying agents of different nature. For example, Armstrong et al. developed a bismuth based electrode of easy preparation and low cost for the stripping analysis of these heavy metals, obtaining high sensitivity with low detection limits (93 and 54 ng L−1 for Pb(II) and Cd(II), respectively) (Armstrong, Tatum, Dansby-Sparks, Chambers, & Xue, 2010). On the other hand, Alam et al. have studied the use of carbon nanomaterials as multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) with β-cyclodextrin (βCD) for the stripping detection of lead, highlighting the various properties of carbon nanostructures such as wide electrical potential window, fast electron-transfer rate and large surface area (Alam, Howlader, Hu, & Deen, 2019). Among the most relevant modifying agents are the nano-allotropic forms of carbon, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and others. Voltammetric analysis of different species using electrode systems based on carbon nanostructures has been explored by various authors. For example, Compton et al. investigated the incorporation of nano-carbon black on the surface of a glassy carbon electrode as an alternative in terms of cost to the traditional multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) for the determination of nicotine by adsorptive stripping voltammetry (AdSV). The results of these authors indicate that the advantages of using these nanomaterials lead to lower background currents and larger Faradaic currents (Lo, Aldous, & Compton, 2012). Recently our research group published an article based on an electrochemical sensor for the simultaneous determination of Cd(II) and Pb(II) by square wave anodic stripping voltammetry (SWASV) in bivalve mollusks using a glassy carbon electrode modified with electrochemically reduced graphene oxide. The high conductivity and greater surface area of the modifying agent improves the preconcentration capacity of the electrochemical sensor, allowing to develop a simple, rapid and sensitive analysis in the detection of lead and cadmium in marine resources (Pizarro, Segura et al., 2019). Nowadays, attention has focused on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) which are thin graphene sheets smaller than 100 nm that confine the electronic movement in the three spatial dimensions (0-D). From a structural point of view, GQDs contain hydrophilic edges associated with the presence of carboxylic acids that give it a high dispersion in water and functionalization capacity with various organic, polymeric, inorganic and biological species. Probably, the most relevant properties of GQDs are its high photoluminescence, large surface area, low cytotoxicity, as well as presenting biocompatibility, allowing its application in bioimage, optoelectronic devices, photoluminescent and electrochemical sensors (Shen, Zhu, Yang, & Li, 2012; Sun, Wu, Wei, & Qu, 2013; Ting, Ee, Ananthanarayanan, Leong, & Chen, 2015)

The development of electrochemical sensors using GQDs is still incipient. For example, Gholivand y col. developed an electrochemical sensor based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) modified with thionine and immobilised as a suitable nanostructure modifier on the nano-porous glassy carbon electrode (npGCE) for selective and sensitive determination of cisplatin. The authors indicate that the use of GQDs contributes significantly to a better electronic transfer allowing the increase of the accumulated target at the modified electrode (Gholivand, Ahmadi, & Mavaei, 2019). Others authors have allowed the determination of hydrogen peroxide (Zhang et al., 2013), dopamine (Li et al., 2016), acetoaminophen, ascorbic acid (Zhao et al., 2015), malachite green dye (Hou, Bei, Wang, & Ai, 2013), hydroquinone and catechol (Jian et al., 2016), however, there are few works associated with the modification of electrodes with GQDs for the determination of heavy metals. Recently Ou et al. (Ou, Tao, Ma, & Kong, 2015) incorporated a composite generated from electrostatic and hydrogen bridge interactions between a biodegradable, non-toxic and biocompatible polysaccharide such as poly-β-(1,4)-2-acetamido-2-desoxy-d-glucopiranose (chitosan) and GQDs for the determination of metals by generating a bismuth film in situ. The results obtained allowed to reach detection limits of 8.84, 1.99 and 3.10 µg L−1 for Zn(II), Cd(II) and Pb(II), respectively. On the other hand, Zhou et al. they modified a surface of GC with GQDs generated from the reduction of GO using l-ascorbic acid and sodium polystyrene sulfonate as dispersing agent for the determination of Pb(II) (Zhou, Jiang, & Via, 2014).

Based on this background, it is of interest, the development of new analytical methodologies for the determination of high toxicity species regulated by international standards present in bivalve mollusks. Considering that the incorporation of carbonaceous materials to a conventional GC electrode can improve the electroanalytical qualities of the system through an increase in the surface area and therefore the mass transport of the analyte, is that in this work, the incorporation of a permeable polymeric resin towards cations (Nafion, NF) is evaluated as an immobilizer of GQDs on GC for the simultaneous determination of Cd(II) and Pb(II) using SWASV.

All of the reagents were analytic grade. Boric acid, formic acid, glacial acetic acid, orthophosphoric acid 85%, standard solutions of selenium (SeO2 in HNO3, 1000 mg L−1), zinc (Zn(NO3)2 in HNO3, 1000 mg L-1), tin (SnCl4 in HCl, 1000 mg L−1) lead (Pb(NO3)2 in H2O, 1000 mg L−1), cadmium (CdCl2 in H2O, 1000 mg L−1), iron (FeCl3 in HCl, 1000 mg L−1), nickel (NiCl2 in H2O, 1000 mg L−1), were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). TMDA-64.2 certified reference material. Spectroscopic measurements were performed on a Perkin Elmer Spectrometer (Lambda 25 UV–Vis spectrophotometer; Waltham, MA, USA) and a spectrofluorometer (Jasco model FP-8300; Oklahoma City, OK, USA). Electrochemical impedance measurements were carried out in the same electrochemical cell with a CHInstruments CHI604C potentiostat at open circuit potential. A sinusoidal voltage perturbation of amplitude 5 mV was applied, scanning in the 10 mHz frequency range with 12 points per frequency decade. The impedance data were analyzed and fitted to electrical equivalent circuits with ZView 3.5c software. All experiments were carried out at room temperature. Microscopic measurements were performed on a Hitachi HT7700TEM and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analyzes were performed using an electron microscope (SEM, FEI Quanta 250). The square wave anodic stripping voltammetry (SWASV) experiments were performed using a CHInstrument (USA) 620C potentiostat. The three-electrode system consisted of a glassy carbon (GC) working electrode (CHI104, 3 mm diameter), a saturated calomel reference electrode (SCE), and a platinum auxiliary electrode in a 10 mL electrochemical cell. The lyophilization process carried out through in a Virtis Personal Advantage Plus Freeze Dryers. Digestion analysis was performed in an Ethos 1 Advanced Microware Digestion System (Milestone)
The preparation of the compound was made from a mixture of 900 µL of GQDs solution and 100 µL of 0.5 wt% Nafion solution that was sonicated for 30 min at room temperature.

The GC electrode was mechanically polished using 0.3 µm alumina with Milli-Q water on a porous surface. The electrochemical cell was washed with a 0.5 mol L−1 HNO3 solution. Using the drop-coating method, a 10 µL aliquot of GQDs-NF was added on GC, allowing dry at 60 °C for 10 min.

All shells were carefully removed with a plastic knife; then, they were lyophilized for 24 h and then ground to a fine powder in an agate mortar. A total of 0.5 g of each sample was taken, and a microwave digestion in a mixture of H2O2 and HNO3 (1:9) was performed (Usero, Morillo, & Gracia, 2005).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, characterization of GQDs and characterization of electrode surfaces by SEM
ce:section.ce:para:
The GQDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of citric acid (Dong et al., 2012) and characterized by UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence, and TEM). Fig. 1
(a) shows the UV–Vis spectrum of GQDs, showing a well-defined band at 361 nm. The spectral position of this band is associated with n → π* type transitions. It should be noted that sp2 hybridization associated with olefinic bonds (CC) present in GQDs can generate π → π*-type transitions at less than 300 nm wavelengths (Ananthanarayanan et al., 2014; Pan, Zhang, Li, & Wu, 2010). On the other hand, citric acid absorbs at >250 nm wavelengths. Since GQDs have a forbidden band between the valence and the conduction bands, they can emit electromagnetic radiation at 472 nm when they are excited at 380 nm as can be seen in their fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 1 b). Fig. 1 c shows the TEM image of GQDs, which present uniform size, reaching a lateral size of approximately 3 nm, these results are similar to those reported in the literature (Ananthanarayanan et al., 2014; Jian et al., 2016). The morphology of the surfaces of (a) GC, (b) GC/NF and (c) GC/GQDs-NF were evaluated by SEM. Fig. 1 (d) shows a smooth and homogeneous unmodified surface of GC, while Fig. 1 (e) presents a homogeneous distribution of NF on the electrode surface. Fig. 1 (f) shows the incorporation of the GQDs-NF compound that demonstrates greater surface roughness and porosity in contrast to GC and GC/NF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of modified surfaces by cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)
ce:section.ce:para: The Fig. 2 shows the different cyclic voltammograms of GC, GC/NF and GC/GQDs-NF in the presence of 1 mmol L−1 of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (1:1) in 0.1 mol L−1 KCl. For GC it is observed the typical Fe(II)/Fe(III) redox process with a ΔEp = Ep ox-Ep red of 0.11 V, while the incorporation of Nafion hinders the Fe(II)/Fe(III) redox process due to the presence of a negative charge in the polymer network preventing the diffusion, mass transfer and electron transfer of the Fe(II)/Fe(III) redox couple. The voltammetric response of GC and GC/NF is in agreement with others articles reported in the literature (Chen et al., 2007; J. Li, Guo, Zhai, & Wang, 2009). The inclusion of GQDs favors the electron transfer and the current intensities of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− redox process compared to GC/NF, presenting a ΔEp = 0.17 V. According to the values obtained in ΔEp, the incorporation of GQDs does not contribute from a thermodynamic point of view to the voltammetric response of the redox mediator, however, the variation in the ΔEp is an indicator of the change in the nature of the surface. Electrical changes in electrode surface due to modifications with NF and GQDs were analyzed by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. Fig. 2 (b) shows the characteristic Nyquist diagram of bare and modified electrodes, where the change in the semicircle diameter is a result of the change in the resistance to the electron transfer (Ret) from the electrode to the Fe(II)/Fe(III) redox couple in solution. The electrode coated with Nafion (0.5 wt%) blocked the electron transfer regarding bare electrode, while electrode coated with Nafion and GQDs significantly decreased Ret respect to GC/NF, but not respect to GC. This demonstrates that the electrochemical activity of GC/GQDs-NF electrode is higher than that of GC/NF electrode. Also, this result agrees with the voltammetric results shown above. The inset shows in detail the semicircle of Ret representing to GC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of chemical and electrochemical parameters
ce:section.ce:para: To perform the analytical methodology, the effect of chemical and electrochemical variables (supporting electrolyte effect, pH, modifying agent concentration, accumulation potential and accumulation time) on the voltammetric response of Cd(II) and Pb(II), which allowed to obtain defined signals from these metal ions.

The Fig. 3
(a) shows the voltammetric profiles for different supporting electrolytes (formate, acetate and Britton-Robinson buffer). When using Britton-Robinson buffer, the current response is disfavor compared to acetate and formate buffers, due to the possibility of precipitate formation with the components of the buffer solution; for example, metal phosphates may precipitate in the Britton-Robinson acid medium buffer (Torma, Kádár, Tóth, & Tatár, 2008). No significant differences in the Cd(II) current signal are observed using acetate and formate buffers, however, for Pb(II) there is an increase in the 24.4% of the current signal using 0.1 mol L−1 of buffer of formate, pH 4.5. Based on these results, 0.1 mol L−1 formate buffer pH 4.5 was chosen for additional measurements. The effect of Nafion concentration on the electrochemical response of 40 µg L−1 of Cd(II) and Pb(II) was studied by depositing a polymer proportion between 0.1 and 2.0 wt% on the electrode surface. Fig. 3 (b) shows an increase on the stripping signal of both species as a function of the Nafion concentration, tending to a maximum of 0.5 wt%. Although the polymer membrane favors preconcentration of cations in an intermediate proportion of Nafion (0.5 wt%), at lower values, the GC surface coating is insufficient by decreasing the current intensity for both analytes (Kefala & Economou, 2006; Porchet & Javet, 1995). The increase in thickness of the polymeric film decreases the diffusion rate of the two species, and therefore, there is a decrease in terms of current (Kefala, Economou, & Voulgaropoulos, 2004; Torma et al., 2008). Therefore, 0.5 wt% of Nafion was chosen as the optimal ratio for subsequent measurements.

Once the Nafion concentration is optimized, the effect of pH on the voltammetric response of 40 µg L−1 of Cd(II) and Pb(II) is evaluated using GC/GQDs-NF between values of 3.0 and 4.5, taking into account the regulatory range of the formate buffer (2.6–4.8) (Perrin & Dempsey, 1974). As seen in Fig. 3 (c), the highest current intensity is obtained at a pH of 4.5. At lower pH values, there is a decrease in current attributed to competition between the analytes and the protons in the medium for the active sites of the electrode surface. Therefore, pH 4.5 was chosen for further studies.

The preconcentration stage of SWASV is governed by analyte reduction potential (Eacc) and the time required to accumulate or reduce species on the electrode surface (tacc). The effect of Eacc on the 40 µg L−1 current signal of Cd(II) and Pb(II) using GC/GQDs-NF was studied between −1.6 and 1.0 V (Fig. 3 (d)). All measurements were performed in triplicate (n = 3) with an RSD of less than 5%. For both species, at potentials lower than −1.0 V there is an increase in the intensity of the current, which reaches a maximum at −1.3 V for Cd(II) and Pb(II). At potentials more negative than −1.3 V, a decrease in the signal current of both species is attributed to the evolution of H2 (Segura et al., 2015). Based on these results, a potential of −1.3 V was chosen as the optimum Eacc.

Fig. 3(e) shows the dependence of the current signal of 40 µg L−1 Cd(II) and Pb(II) using GC/GQDs-NF with the accumulation time. There is an increase in the current signal of both species as tacc increases, reaching a maximum in 180 s. A plateau is then observed that is associated with an adsorption equilibrium between the surface and the analytes. To avoid rapid saturation of the active GC/GQDs-NF sites, a tacc of 150 s was chosen to develop analytical validation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of Cd(II) and Pb(II) and analytical validation
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 4
(a) shows the voltammetric profile of 40 μg L−1 Cd(II) and Pb(II) obtaining with GC signals of 0.12 Cd(II) and 2.96 μA Pb(II), GC/NF 0.54 μA Cd(II) and 3.08 μA Pb(II), representing an increase of more than 100% in the case of Cd(II) and an increase of 4.1% for Pb(II). On the other hand, GC/GQDs-NF showed signals of 1.06 μA Cd(II) and 3.93 μA Pb(II), showing a percentage increase of 96.3% and 27.6% for Cd(II) and Pb(II), respectively, compared to the GC/NF system. The differences found between GC and GC/NF are attributed to the selective permeability of the Nafion membrane to positively charged species. On the other hand, the increase in current in GC/GQDs-NF for both species compared to GC and GC/NF is attributed to the increase in the number of active sites provided by GQDs. It was not possible to evaluate the GC/GQDs electrode due to that under the experimental conditions studied, these carbon nanostructures present high dispersion in aqueous matrices and detach from the electrode surface, preventing a reproducible analysis.

The analytical methodology for the determination of Cd(II) and Pb(II) using GC/GQDs-NF performed in sodium formate buffer pH 4.5, Eacc = -1.3 V, and tacc = 150 s. The Fig. 4 (b) shows the voltammograms and the calibration curve obtained for GC/GQDs-NF. For both species, there is a linearity range of 20–200 µg L−1. The calibration range both for Cd(II) and Pb(II) taking into account the dilution in the sample pretreatment is 1.0–10.0 mg Kg−1. For Cd(II) a linear equation of Y = 0.0254 (±0.0008)x + 0.5757 (±0.1037), R2 = 0.992, was obtained, with a sensibility of 0.0254 µA µg−1 L, while for Pb(II) the linear equation was Y = 0.1769 (±0.0040)x – 2.7420 (±0.5006), R2 = 0.995, obtaining a sensibility of 0.1769 µA µg−1 L. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 3 times the standard deviation of the intercept on the slope of the calibration curve, indicating a LOD of 11.30 μg L−1 for Cd(II) and 8.49 μg L−1 for Pb(II), respectively. The obtained results are comparable to the values reported in the literature. An acceptable reproducibility was obtained using four different electrodes, expressed in terms of the relative standard deviation of 40 µg L−1 of Cd(II) and Pb(II) with values of 4.05 and 9.56%, respectively.

The possible interference of Sn(II), Se(IV), Ni(II), Fe(III), and Zn(II) on the simultaneous determination of Cd(II) and Pb(II) was studied individually by the addition of 10 and 100 µg L−1 of each cation under optimized conditions. The stripping signal of Cd(II) and Pb(II) was almost not affected by ±10%, with the exception of Fe(III), which generated a decrease of 35.1% for Cd(II), while Zn(II) generated a decrease greater than 10% for Cd(II) at both 10 and 100 µg L−1. This interference is attributed to two factors: competition between the analytes and the interfering ions for the active sites of GC/GQDs-NF and the formation of intermetallic compounds. In spite of the current variations associated with these interferers, when the standard addition method was applied in the simultaneous determination the interferer’s effect is minimized, allowing the determination of Cd(II) and Pb(II) in complex matrices.

The accuracy of the methodology was evaluated from the standard addition method using GC/GQDs-NF (under optimized conditions) in the analysis of a certified reference material (TMDA-64.2) containing a known concentration of 264 and 286 µg L−1 for Cd(II) and Pb (II), respectively and other species in a concentration range of 0.08 to 635 µg L−1). To perform the analysis, the sample was diluted with sodium formate buffer (pH 4.5). Fig. 5 shows the different voltammograms and standard addition curve for Cd(II) and Pb(II). It is interesting to note that GC/GQDs-NF display a well-defined stripping signal, however the effect of the other species present in TMDA-64.2 translates into a variation of the stripping potential values of Cd(II) and Pb(II) compared to the normal calibration curve (Fig. 4 b). However the results obtained indicate that, despite the high degree of interfering potentials, it is possible to simultaneously detect and quantify these metals with relative standard deviation (RSD) values of 5.16 and 9.27% for Cd(II) and Pb(II), respectively, with respect to certified by TMDA-64.2.

GC/GQDs-NF was applied to determine Cd(II) and Pb(II) in two samples of bivalve mollusks (n = 3) using the standard addition method. The samples were analyzed by SWASV and inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) without detecting the presence of Cd(II) in both samples (Table 1 ). The values obtained for Pb(II) in both methods are statistically comparable, with RSD less than 10%. Another way to check if the results obtained by two different methods do not differ significantly, is to estimate a conjunct standard deviation from the individual standard deviations of each method. Since the experimental value of |t| is less than the critical value of |t| (4.3) for two degrees of freedom (P = 0.05) no significant statistical difference can be seen between the means demonstrating that the proposed method can be used effectively for the determination of Pb(II) in real bivalve samples.

On the other hand, the results showed that the levels of these species are not within the allowed levels established by international organizations such as the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) (Nauen, 1983).

The results obtained by GC/GQDs-NF were compared with different electrochemical systems based on graphene for the determination of Cd(II) and Pb(II) (Table 2 ). From an electrochemical point of view, in general, the parameters obtained of potential and accumulation time are similar for each system, with no significant differences. Although other articles show lower detection limits, the vast majority of these works focus their analysis on aqueous samples that do not require prior treatment.

A very inexpensive and environmental friendly electrode was optimized for the simultaneous determination of Cd(II) and Pb(II) in seafood. The GC electrode modified with graphene quantum dots stabilized by Nafion shows very good results when compared with certified samples and ICP-OES method. The developed electrode system was successfully applied for the simultaneous determination of Cd(II) and Pb(II) at the trace level in bivalve mollusks using SWASV. Under optimized conditions, a linearity range of 20–200 µg L−1 with a LOD of 11.30 μg L−1 for Cd(II) and 8.49 μg L−1 for Pb(II), respectively.

Jaime Pizarro and Rodrigo Segura. Conceived, designed and wrote the paper. Diego Tapia, Freddy Navarro and Francesca Fuenzalida contributed the literature study and performed the experiments. María Jesús Aguirre. Analyzed the results and reviewed the paper. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank FONDECYT Chile, for financial support under projects N° 1180804 (RS), 1160324 (MJA), “Comisión Nacional de Investigación Científica y Tecnológica” (CONICYT) for doctoral fellowship 21150868 (FN) and to the project 1145-USA 1799-1-1-3 VRIDEI, Universidad de Santiago de Chile.
